confirmed_bugs.reasoning: Verified by reading and by execution. log_writer.go:123 emits the base-time header with a lowercase 't': `fmt.Fprintf(lw.log, "#[Basetime: %d (seconds since epoch)]\n", secs)`. The reader's regex at log_reader.go:55 is `#\[BaseTime: ([\d\.]*)` (capital 'T'), compiled with regexp.Compile which is case-sensitive by default (no `(?i)` flag). In readIntervalHistogram (log_reader.go:114-121) the base-time branch runs `hlr.reBaseTime.FindStringSubmatch(line)`; against the writer's "#[Basetime:" line this returns no match, so observedBaseTime stays false and baseTimeSec stays 0. A relative-timestamp log written by this library thus loses its base time on read-back: the reader falls into the fallback deduction path at lines 155-165 and computes wrong absolute timestamps. The writer's own doc comment (log_writer.go:120) says the line "starts with the leading text '#[BaseTime:'", contradicting its actual output, and the correct casing also matches the Java HdrHistogram convention. My throwaway test wrote 3600000 ms via OutputBaseTime, produced the line "#[Basetime: 3600 (seconds since epoch)]\n", fed the buffer to NewHistogramLogReader, and observed baseTimeSec=0 / observedBaseTime=false instead of 3600/true — the test failed on current code, confirming the round-trip is broken. One-line fix: change the writer format string to "#[BaseTime: ...".

Traced hdr_encoding.go Decode (lines 53-78). After base64 decode succeeds (line 55-58), line 59 `rbuf := bytes.NewBuffer(decoded[0:8])` slices the first 8 bytes unconditionally with NO length check. The only length validation (lines 71-75) happens AFTER this slice. So any input that base64-decodes to fewer than 8 bytes triggers a slice-out-of-range panic. Proven by execution: Decode([]byte("")) -> "panic: runtime error: slice bounds out of range [:8] with capacity 0". Decode is an exported public API taking arbitrary external/untrusted input (archival/transmission per the doc comment), so a panic here is a real availability/robustness bug, not a mere internal invariant. Fix: guard len(decoded) < 8 before slicing and return an error.

Traced hdr_encoding.go Decode. Line 66 `lengthOfCompressedContents := r32[1]` is a signed int32 read directly from attacker-controlled bytes. Line 71 computes `decodeLengthOfCompressedContents := int32(len(decoded[8:]))` (non-negative). Line 72 guard `if lengthOfCompressedContents > decodeLengthOfCompressedContents` only rejects too-large values; a negative length passes it (e.g. -1 > 4 is false). Line 76 then slices `decoded[8:8+lengthOfCompressedContents]` = `decoded[8:7]`, an invalid low>high slice, which panics. Decode is a public API that parses untrusted base64 input, so this is a reachable DoS. Proven by execution: crafted header with cookie=compressedEncodingCookie (0x1c849314) and length=int32(-1), base64-encoded, passed to Decode — panics with `runtime error: slice bounds out of range [8:7]`. Test failed on current code exactly as claimed. Real, unhandled.

Traced and proved by execution. `Decode` (hdr_encoding.go:53) is a public API that decodes untrusted base64/zlib input. It calls decodeCompressedFormat -> fillCountsArrayFromSourceBuffer (hdr_encoding.go:200-221). That loop advances `dstIndex` from decoded varints and, on any positive count, calls `rh.setCountAtIndex(int(dstIndex), count)` with NO bound against len(rh.counts). setCountAtIndex (hdr.go:315-318) is just `h.counts[idx] += n` — no guard. The countsLen/`value too large` guard exists only in RecordValues (hdr.go:305-311), which this decode path bypasses entirely. A payload with more positive varints than countsLen (or a negative zero-run that pushes dstIndex past the end before a single positive write) writes out of range. I built a structurally valid blob (encodingCookie | payloadLen | offset0 | sig1 | lowest=1 | highest=2 | ratio1.0, countsLen=32, followed by 37 zigzag(1) varints), zlib-compressed and wrapped it with the outer compressed cookie + length, base64-encoded, and called Decode: it panics with `runtime error: index out of range [32] with length 32`. Trivially reachable DoS on the public deserialization API from attacker-controlled data.
Confirmed by execution.
confirmed_bugs.fix: test panics on current code

confirmed_bugs.reasoning: Traced and proved by execution. Decode -> decodeCompressedFormat (hdr_encoding.go:165) reads NumberOfSignificantValueDigits, LowestTrackableValue, HighestTrackableValue straight from the decompressed header (line 181) and passes them to New (line 194). New (hdr.go:83) clamps only sig to [1,5] and lowest to >=1; it does NOT bound highestTrackableValue. countsLen=(bucketCount+1)*(subBucketCount/2): sig=5 gives subBucketCount=262144 (subBucketHalfCount=131072), highest=MaxInt64 gives ~47 buckets, so countsLen=6,160,384 -> make([]int64, countsLen) = ~47 MB (hdr.go:138). The only size validation in decode (hdr_encoding.go:190) checks PayloadLength==actualPayloadLen but never ties geometry to payload length, so a header with PayloadLength=0 and an empty payload passes while forcing the 47 MB allocation. Proof: I built New(1, MaxInt64, 5) with no recorded values, encoded it (V2 compressed) -> base64 input was only 56 bytes; Decode of it allocated 49,330,296 bytes (47 MB), an ~880,000x amplification. An attacker feeding bytes to Decode/Import can drive large per-call heap allocation from tiny inputs (memory-amplification DoS). Real gap: no ceiling relating countsLen (or highest/sig) to actual payload byte count before allocating. Severity medium is fair: needs attacker-controlled decode input, bounded at ~47 MB per call by the sig<=5 clamp, but repeated/concurrent decodes multiply it. A sound tight bound is countsLen<=actualPayloadLen (each non-zero count and each zero-run token consumes >=1 payload byte).

hdr.go:229 in Mean() computes `i.countAtIdx*h.medianEquivalentValue(i.valueFromIdx)` as an int64*int64 product BEFORE the float64 cast: `mean += float64(i.countAtIdx*h.medianEquivalentValue(i.valueFromIdx)) / totalCount`. Both operands are int64. When count*value exceeds int64 max (9.223e18) the product wraps silently and the cast to float64 propagates the garbage. This is reachable: a histogram with a large trackable range (or a normal microsecond-range histogram at high volume) easily produces count*value > 9.2e18. Proven by execution: New(1, 1e15, 3) with RecordValues(1e15, 100000) yields an int64 product of 9.99e19 which wraps to 7.739e18; Mean() returns 7.739e13 instead of the correct ~9.997e14 — off by ~10x, silently. The upstream Java HdrHistogram casts count to double first (`(double) count * median`) so it does the arithmetic in float64 and never overflows. Note StdDev() at line 249 already does it correctly (`... * float64(i.countAtIdx) / totalCount`), so Mean is the sole offender. One-line fix: multiply as floats.

In hdr_encoding.go Decode(), line 55 base64-decodes the input into `decoded`, and line 59 immediately does `rbuf := bytes.NewBuffer(decoded[0:8])` with NO length check on `decoded`. When the decoded byte slice has capacity < 8, the slice expression `decoded[0:8]` panics with runtime error "slice bounds out of range [:8] with capacity N" instead of returning an error. The first length-related guard (line 71-75, comparing len(decoded[8:]) to lengthOfCompressedContents) is unreachable because the panic happens six lines earlier. I proved this by running Decode against three inputs through a throwaway external program (no tracked files modified): Decode([]byte("")) -> "PANIC: slice bounds out of range [:8] with capacity 0"; base64 of {0x1c,0x84} (decodes to 2 bytes) -> "PANIC: ... capacity 3"; a 7-byte payload happened not to panic (its decode buffer capacity was >=8) and instead returned a normal error. So any attacker-controlled or malformed base64 header shorter than 8 decoded bytes with capacity<8 crashes the process. This is a real reachable panic on the public Decode API (a DoS on untrusted input), correctly classified high. The one-line fix is to check len(decoded) < 8 (equivalently the ENCODING_HEADER_SIZE header) before slicing and return an error.

Traced and reproduced by execution. In Decode (hdr_encoding.go), line 66 reads `lengthOfCompressedContents := r32[1]` as a signed int32 straight from attacker-controlled input. The only bounds check is line 72: `if lengthOfCompressedContents > decodeLengthOfCompressedContents` — it rejects values LARGER than the trailing buffer but never rejects negative values. A negative length sails through. Line 76 then evaluates `decoded[8:8+lengthOfCompressedContents]`; with lengthOfCompressedContents = -1 this is `decoded[8:7]`, low(8) > high(7), an unrecoverable runtime panic (slice bounds out of range [8:7]).

Reachability is satisfied: the cookie guard at line 67 needs `r32[0] & ^0xf0 == 0x1c849304`. Setting the 4-byte cookie to int32(0x1c849304|0x10)=0x1c849314 masks to 0x1c849304, so it passes. The 9-byte buffer (8-byte header + 1 trailing byte) makes decoded[0:8] valid and gives decodeLengthOfCompressedContents=1, so -1 > 1 is false and the guard is bypassed.

I built exactly that input, base64-encoded it, and called Decode — it panics at hdr_encoding.go:76 with `slice bounds out of range [8:7]` (test output above). This is a DoS on any caller that decodes untrusted/serialized histograms without a recover(), which the public Decode API invites. Real, high-severity.

Fix is one line: also reject negative lengths in the guard.

Traced log_reader.go lines 126-129 exactly. When a line starts with "Tag=" but has no comma, `commaPos := strings.Index(line, ",")` returns -1, then `tag = line[4:commaPos]` evaluates to `line[4:-1]`, which panics with "slice bounds out of range [:-1]". There is no guard between line 127 and 128. I proved it by dropping a throwaway test into the repo, running `go test`, and observing the panic originate at log_reader.go:128 (stack trace shows decodeNextIntervalHistogram -> NextIntervalHistogramWithRange -> NextIntervalHistogram). The panic is an unrecovered runtime panic that crosses the public API boundary (NextIntervalHistogram), so any malformed log with a "Tag=" line lacking a comma crashes the caller instead of returning an error. Severity is medium rather than high: it requires parsing an untrusted/malformed log file, is a crash (DoS) not memory corruption, and the surrounding function otherwise returns errors gracefully; still a real reachable panic on attacker/user-controlled input.

Traced and reproduced by execution (panic observed at hdr_encoding.go:59). The interval regex reHistogramInterval = `([\d\.]*),([\d\.]*),([\d\.]*),(.*)` (log_reader.go:60) has group 4 = `(.*)`, which matches the empty string. For input line "1.0,2.0,3.0,\n": matchRes[1]="1.0",[2]="2.0",[3]="3.0",[4]="" -> cpayload="" (log_reader.go:144). Default NextIntervalHistogram uses range [0, math.MaxFloat64] absolute (line 80). observedStartTime false => startTimeSec=1.0, baseTimeSec=0.0, absoluteStartTimeStampSec=1.0, passing both range checks (1.0>=0 and 1.0<=MaxFloat64), so Decode([]byte("")) runs at log_reader.go:187. In Decode (hdr_encoding.go:53), base64.StdEncoding.DecodeString("") returns empty []byte with nil error, so no early return; `decoded[0:8]` at hdr_encoding.go:59 panics: slice bounds out of range [:8] with capacity 0. The panic is unrecovered and crashes the log reader. Same bug class exists at hdr_encoding.go:259 (decoded[0:40]). Real high-severity DoS on a malformed-but-valid .hlog line.

Traced the real code path. Decode (hdr_encoding.go:76) calls decodeCompressedFormat(..., ENCODING_HEADER_SIZE=40). At line 176 io.ReadAll(z) returns decompressedSlice; line 181 immediately passes decompressedSlice[0:headerSize] (=[0:40]) into decodeDeCompressedHeaderFormat, which at line 259 does decoded[0:40] and binary.Read's a 40-byte header. There is NO check that len(decompressedSlice) >= 40 anywhere before these slices.

I proved the claim by execution (throwaway test, now removed). Crafting a valid outer V2Compressed blob whose zlib stream decompresses to 0 bytes: Decode returns "encoding not supported... got 0 want 478450435" — the cookie is read from the zeroed tail of io.ReadAll's over-allocated (cap>=512) buffer, not from real data, and no truncated-header error is produced. With a 10-byte decompressed payload "0123456789", the parsed cookie is 808530435 == 0x30313233 == "0123", proving the header is assembled from the 10 real bytes PLUS 30 zeroed capacity bytes. So the finding's central factual claims are all CONFIRMED: no length check, and header fields are silently read from zeroed capacity rather than erroring on truncation.

Two caveats that lower severity from the claimed medium to low: (1) No panic is reachable today. io.ReadAll starts at make([]byte,0,512), so cap is always >= 512 for any sub-40-byte result; the slice expression s[0:40] requires only high<=cap, so it never panics. The line-195 low>high panic and the "cap==len<40" case are purely hypothetical (a speculative future stdlib change), i.e. PLAUSIBLE-only, not a current bug. (2) The in-capacity read is memory-safe in Go — no out-of-bounds access to unowned memory, no information leak (the capacity bytes are freshly zeroed by make, not stale). So the concrete, real defect is a defensive/robustness gap: malformed (truncated) compressed input is parsed against zeroed memory and yields a misleading error instead of a clean "truncated header" error. Real gap, worth a one-line guard, but low impact.

Traced and proved by execution. hdr.go:169-171 `getNormalizingIndexOffset()` unconditionally returns the constant 1. hdr_encoding.go:138 writes that value into the V2 serialized header at bytes 8-11 (`binary.Write(toCompress, BigEndian, h.getNormalizingIndexOffset())`). I built a throwaway test that encodes `New(1,100000,3)` after `RecordValue(42)`, base64-decodes, strips the 8-byte outer cookie/length header, zlib-inflates the inner payload, and reads inner[8:12] BigEndian: it is 1, not 0. Test fails as designed.

Why Go itself survives: the decoder ignores the field — `decodeCompressedFormat` (hdr_encoding.go:181) captures the offset into `_`, and `fillCountsArrayFromSourceBuffer` (hdr_encoding.go:200-217) writes counts at the raw logical `dstIndex` via `setCountAtIndex`, never applying any offset. So a Go->Go round trip is lossless and the bug is invisible in-process.

Why it is a real interop corruption: the HdrHistogram wire format defines normalizingIndexOffset as a field the reader must apply when mapping logical bucket index to physical storage. The in-workspace C reference confirms this: apply_to_counts_zz (src/hdr_histogram_log.c) fills h->counts[i] at logical i while offset is still 0, then sets normalizing_index_offset from the header; subsequent user reads go through counts_get_normalised -> normalize_index (hdr_histogram.c), which for offset=1 maps every logical index i to physical i-1 (wrapping). A C/Java reader ingesting a Go-produced blob therefore shifts every populated bucket by one slot, so per-bucket counts and hdr_value_at_percentile land one bucket off, and any re-encode propagates the corruption. Emitting a nonzero offset with counts stored at unshifted logical positions is internally inconsistent with the format contract. The correct value for a never-rotated/never-normalized histogram is 0.

Traced Decode() in /home/fco/redislabs/hdr-agent-workspace/hdrhistogram-go/hdr_encoding.go. After base64.StdEncoding.DecodeString succeeds, line 59 does `rbuf := bytes.NewBuffer(decoded[0:8])` with NO length check on `decoded`. Any valid-base64 payload that decodes to <8 bytes triggers a slice-bounds-out-of-range panic before the length is ever validated. Decode is an exported entrypoint intended for untrusted input (log files / network per the package doc), so this is a DoS. I proved it by execution: a throwaway test decoding base64 of {1,2,3,4} (6 decoded bytes) panicked with `panic: runtime error: slice bounds out of range [:8] with capacity 6` at hdr_encoding.go:59, matching the claim exactly. A 0-byte payload hits the same path with capacity 0. Note base64.DecodeString itself rejects malformed base64 with an error (the err!=nil return at line 56 guards that), but well-formed short base64 sails through to the unguarded slice. Real bug.

Traced hdr_encoding.go:53-77. `lengthOfCompressedContents := r32[1]` (line 66) is a signed int32 read via binary.Read. The only guard is line 72 `if lengthOfCompressedContents > decodeLengthOfCompressedContents`, a one-sided upper-bound check. A negative value (e.g. -1) is NOT greater than the buffer length, so the guard is skipped and execution reaches line 76 `decodeCompressedFormat(decoded[8:8+lengthOfCompressedContents], ...)` where the slice high bound becomes 8+(-1)=7 < 8 (low), producing an unrecovered runtime panic. I reproduced it: crafting a base64 frame with BE cookie=compressedEncodingCookie (0x1c849314, which masks to the accepted base 0x1c849304), BE int32 length=-1, and 8 trailing zero bytes, then calling Decode, panics with exactly `slice bounds out of range [8:7]` at hdr_encoding.go:76. Decode operates on fully untrusted/external base64 input (archival/transmission), so an attacker-supplied blob crashes the process. The C reference rejects negative lengths explicitly (hdr_histogram_log.c: `compressed_length < 0`), confirming the intended contract. Real, remotely-triggerable DoS.

Traced and executed. hdr_encoding.go:200-221 fillCountsArrayFromSourceBuffer loops over the zig-zag payload with `var dstIndex int64 = 0` and never bounds-checks it: for count>=0 it calls rh.setCountAtIndex(int(dstIndex), count), and setCountAtIndex (hdr.go:315) blindly does `h.counts[idx] += n` with NO guard (the only bounds check in the codebase lives in RecordValues at hdr.go:307, not on this decode path). For count<0 it does `dstIndex += -count` (zeros-run) equally unchecked. This is reachable from untrusted input via the public Decode (hdr_encoding.go:53) → decompress → fillCountsArrayFromSourceBuffer. I ran a test: a 100000-byte payload of 0x02 (each = zig-zag +1) into New(1,1000,3) panics with `runtime error: index out of range [2048] with length 2048`. The C reference (apply_to_counts_zz) guards with counts_index < counts_len and counts_index + zeros > counts_len, returning HDR_TRAILING_ZEROS_INVALID; the Go port dropped that guard. Real DoS: any caller decoding attacker-supplied HdrHistogram frames can be crashed. Confirmed by execution, not just reading.

Traced hdr_encoding.go. Decode() (line 76) calls decodeCompressedFormat(decoded[8:8+len], ENCODING_HEADER_SIZE=40). Inside (line 176) decompressedSlice = io.ReadAll(z); at line 181 it immediately does decompressedSlice[0:headerSize] with NO length check, and decodeDeCompressedHeaderFormat re-slices decoded[0:40] (line 259). There is no `if len(decompressedSlice) < headerSize` guard anywhere before parsing lowest/highest/sig. I reproduced it: a valid zlib body of 5 bytes {01 02 03 04 05} makes Decode read past the real data — it parsed cookie=16909060 (=0x01020304, the first 4 real bytes) plus zeroed capacity for the remaining 36 header bytes, then returned the MISLEADING error "encoding not supported ... got 16909060 want 478450435" instead of a clean "decompressed data too short". So the over-read into slice capacity and the misleading-error behavior are REAL and demonstrated. Two caveats that lower severity from the medium claim: (1) the panic scenario is NOT reachable on this path — decompressedSlice always comes from io.ReadAll, which allocates with cap>=512, so 40<512 never exceeds cap; a caller cannot supply a smaller-capacity buffer here. (2) Go zeroes fresh allocations, so the over-read yields zero bytes, not stale/leaked heap. Net effect: robustness gap producing a confusing error on malformed input, no crash and no info leak. Hence CONFIRMED but low, not medium.

Traced log_reader.go:96-103. bufio.Reader.ReadString('
') returns the buffered partial line together with io.EOF when the stream ends without a trailing newline. The loop handles io.EOF by `err = nil; break` and NEVER inspects the returned `line`, so a final interval record with no trailing '
' is discarded. Confirmed by running a throwaway test in-repo: writing one valid interval histogram, isolating its interval line, then feeding it to NewHistogramLogReader. With a trailing '
' NextIntervalHistogram returns a non-nil histogram; without the trailing '
' it returns (nil, nil) — the record is silently dropped. Output: "with newline: h=true err=<nil>" vs "without newline: h=false err=<nil>". This diverges from the Java/C readers and affects truncated/concatenated logs. Not already handled anywhere in the loop.

I reproduced both claimed races with `go test -race` against the current, unmodified code.

Front (a) — the `w.Current` pointer field. `Rotate()` writes it unsynchronized at window.go:43 (`w.Current = &w.h[w.idx%len(w.h)]`), while a recorder reads `w.Current` to call `RecordValue`. Race detector reported: "Read ... TestWindowedRotateRaceXX.func1 (record site)" vs "Previous write ... (*WindowedHistogram).Rotate window.go:43". Exactly as claimed.

Front (b) — the backing `counts[]` array. `Rotate()` calls `w.Current.Reset()` (window.go:44 → hdr.go:261 `h.counts[i] = 0`) on a bucket that, once the ring wraps (`w.idx % len(w.h)`), is the same *Histogram a recorder is still incrementing via `setCountAtIndex` (hdr.go:316 `h.counts[idx] += n`). Race detector reported: "Read ... setCountAtIndex hdr.go:316 / RecordValues hdr.go:310" vs "Previous write ... Reset hdr.go:261". Exactly as claimed. `totalCount` (hdr.go:260 vs 317) races the same way.

So the technical claim is fully accurate and proven, not merely plausible. The record-in-one-goroutine / Rotate-on-a-ticker pattern is the whole point of WindowedHistogram, and it cannot be done safely: there is zero synchronization in the package (no `sync`/`atomic` usage anywhere; grep confirms). Consequences are lost increments, torn/stale reads, and zeroed-mid-record buckets — statistics corruption, though not memory-unsafety (idx is bounds-checked at hdr.go:308, the pointer is always a valid element of w.h, so no OOB/crash).

Why medium, not high: this is an inherent, library-wide non-thread-safety property (even two goroutines calling RecordValue on a single Histogram race identically), and the package never documents or promises concurrency safety. It is a real, demonstrable API-safety gap for the type's advertised usage, but it degrades to corrupted stats rather than a crash or security issue, and a "one-line fix" as the finding suggests is not sufficient — a correct fix needs an atomic pointer for Current plus a mutex/atomic counters (a design change), so I scope it as a documented-contract / API-hardening finding.

Every factual claim checks out against the code. (1) `grep -niE "thread|concurren|safe|lock|atomic|race|mutex" README.md` returns nothing. (2) The package doc (hdr.go:1-3) and the Histogram type comment (hdr.go:29-31) say nothing about concurrency; window.go:3 likewise. (3) No file imports `sync` — the library offers no synchronized/atomic variant. RecordValue -> RecordValues -> setCountAtIndex (hdr.go:268/310/316) does a plain read-modify-write of the shared counts slice plus totalCount/min/max with zero synchronization. I proved misuse is a genuine data race, not hypothetical: a 4-goroutine RecordValue test run under `go test -race` reports "WARNING: DATA RACE" at setCountAtIndex (hdr.go:316), read vs previous write on the same counts element. So the type is in fact unsafe for concurrent use, the docs are silent, and callers who assume otherwise get silent count corruption. This is a real, verifiable documentation/contract gap. I rate it low rather than medium because it is a doc omission, not a code defect: Go's near-universal convention is that a type is NOT safe for concurrent use unless its docs explicitly say so, so an idiomatic Go caller already assumes external synchronization is required. The gap is worth closing (the Java upstream ships AtomicHistogram/Recorder and WindowedHistogram's timer-rotate use case does invite the racy pattern), but the "callers will reasonably misuse them" premise is weaker for a Go audience than the finding implies.

Traced hdr.go directly. ValueAtPercentilesSlice (line 471) clamps both ends: `if percentile > 100 { percentile = 100 } else if percentile < 0 { percentile = 0 }` (lines 480-484). The three siblings clamp only the HIGH end: ValueAtPercentile (line 339 `if percentile > 100 { percentile = 100 }`, no low clamp) and ValueAtPercentiles (line 424, same). ValueAtQuantile (line 327) just forwards to ValueAtPercentile, so it inherits the gap. With a negative percentile, line 343/428 computes countAtPercentile = int64((neg/100)*total+0.5) which is negative; getValueFromIdxUpToCount's first `countToIdx(0) >= negative` is immediately true at flat index 0, returning valueFromFlatIndex(0). Because percentile != 0.0, line 348 returns highestEquivalentValue(that value) instead of lowestEquivalentValue. Proven by execution on New(1000,1000000,3) with 1000x RecordValue(500000): ValueAtPercentile(-5)=511, ValueAtQuantile(-1)=511, ValueAtPercentiles([-5])[-5]=511, while ValueAtPercentilesSlice([-5])[0]=0. The maintainer's own fix in the slice variant establishes 0 as the intended clamp-to-0th-percentile behavior; the other three diverge. Negative percentile is technically out-of-contract ([0..100]), which is why this is medium rather than high, but the four siblings are documented identically yet behave inconsistently — a real, reproducible defect.

Traced and executed against the real code in /home/fco/redislabs/hdr-agent-workspace/hdrhistogram-go/hdr.go.

At percentile 0.0, line 343 computes countAtPercentile = int64((0/100)*totalCount + 0.5) = 0. getValueFromIdxUpToCount(0) (lines 358-397) enters the scan with countToIdx=0. The first block passes the skip test `countToIdx+s >= 0` (always true, counts are non-negative), then the inner loop at j=0 does countToIdx += counts[0]; since counts[0] is the sub-bucket-0 slot (count 0 for a value like 777) countToIdx stays 0 and the test `countToIdx >= 0` (line 382) is immediately true, returning valueFromFlatIndex(0). valueFromFlatIndex(0) (lines 400-408) yields bucketIdx=0, subBucketIdx=0 => valueFromIndex(0,0) = 0. lowestEquivalentValue(0)=0. So p0 returns 0, never reaching the first populated index. The `if percentile == 0.0` branch (line 345) is effectively dead for the min: it lowest-equivalents a value that is already 0. Same logic in ValueAtPercentiles (line 428 target=0, scan line 446 `total >= 0` fires at idx 0) and ValueAtPercentilesSlice (line 485 target=0, nextTarget=0, line 504 `total >= 0` fires at idx 0).

Executed proof: New(1,1000000,3) with 100x RecordValue(777) gives Min()=777 but ValueAtPercentile(0)=0, ValueAtPercentilesSlice([0])=[0], ValueAtPercentiles([0])=map[0:0]. This deviates from the documented contract (lines 331-332: "largest value that (100%-percentile) of the entries are larger than or equivalent to" — at p0 that is the minimum) and from Java HdrHistogram, which clamps countAtPercentile = max(countAtPercentile, 1) precisely so p0 returns the min. Severity medium: it is a genuine, silent wrong-value deviation from the contract, but only affects the exact p=0.0 query (all other percentiles are unaffected, and Min() is available).

Traced hdr.go:ValueAtPercentiles (lines 418-458). The init loop (line 423) does `for i, percentile := range percentiles`; the clamp `if percentile > 100 { percentile = 100 }` (424-425) mutates only the loop-local copy, never percentiles[i]. It seeds values[percentile]=0 with the CLAMPED key (100) and computes countAtPercentiles[i] from the clamped value. But the resolution scan (line 447) does `currentPercentile := percentiles[pos]`, re-reading the UNMUTATED input slice (still 150), and line 452 writes values[currentPercentile]. So two distinct keys land in the map: the phantom clamped key 100 (still 0, never overwritten since the scan never uses key 100) and the original out-of-range key 150. Proven by execution: New(1,100000,3) with 1000x RecordValue(1000)+RecordValue(50000); ValueAtPercentiles([150]) returns map[100:0 150:50015] (len 2). Sibling ValueAtPercentile(150) clamps and returns 50015 (value at p100), so the correct single-entry answer is map[100:50015]. Negative inputs (no lower clamp exists) similarly leak an illegal key: ValueAtPercentiles([-5,50]) = map[-5:0 50:1000] (len 2). Minor note: finding's stated negative value (-5:511) differs from actual (-5:0), but the phantom/illegal-key defect is exactly as described. Root cause and repro confirmed.

Traced hdr.go:RecordValues (lines 298-313). The only validation is the unsigned index/range check at line 307 (`uint(idx) >= uint(len(h.counts))`), which guards memory safety, not the sign of n. setCountAtIndex (315-318) blindly does `h.counts[idx] += n; h.totalCount += n`. There is no `n <= 0` or `n < 0` check anywhere on the path. The doc (296-297) frames the API as "records n occurrences," implying n>=0, but a negative n is accepted and returns nil.

Proven by execution (throwaway test, now removed): New(1,100000,3); RecordValue(500) -> TotalCount==1; RecordValues(500,-1) returned nil and drove TotalCount to 0. A second run with RecordValues(500,-5) produced TotalCount==-5, i.e. a negative cumulative count. Negative counts/totalCount corrupt every downstream percentile/Mean/StdDev computation, all of which assume monotonically non-negative cumulative sums. n==0 is a harmless no-op that still returns nil.

Severity is low rather than medium: n is caller-supplied and there is no internal call site that can produce a negative n (countsIndexFor returns an index, not n), so this is strictly a caller-misuse / contract-hardening gap with no memory-unsafety and no reachable path from normal library usage. It is nonetheless a real, silent state-corruption gap with a trivial fix.

Traced hdr.go directly. New() (lines 139-141) initializes startTimeMs=0, endTimeMs=0, tag="". Reset() (lines 258-263) only sets h.totalCount=0 and zeroes counts[]; it never touches h.tag, h.startTimeMs, or h.endTimeMs. The doc comment (lines 256-257) states Reset "restores the histogram to its original state." A New() histogram has tag=""/0/0, so after SetTag/SetStartTimeMs/SetEndTimeMs followed by Reset, the accessors Tag()/StartTimeMs()/EndTimeMs() (lines 50-71) still return the mutated values, not the original ones. These fields are meaningful because hdr_histogram_log serialization emits tag and start/end timestamps, so a reset-and-reused histogram carries stale metadata. Real gap; severity low since it affects metadata, not recorded-value correctness.

Traced hdr_benchmark_test.go exactly. In BenchmarkHistogramValueAtPercentileGivenPercentileSlice (lines 61-65) and BenchmarkHistogramValueAtPercentilesGivenPercentileSlice (lines 83-87), the code does `quantiles := make([]float64, b.N)` then `for i := range quantiles { data[i] = rand.Float64() * 100.0 }`. But `data` is returned by populateHistogramLogNormalDist with `data := make([]float64, totalDatapoints)` (line 134), totalDatapoints=1000000. The fill loop iterates b.N times (len of quantiles) yet writes into `data[i]`, so when b.N > 1e6 it panics with index out of range. Meanwhile the timed loops only iterate over the fixed `percentilesOfInterest` slice, so both `quantiles` and the `data[i]` fills are dead — the written values are never read. Reproduced by execution: `go test -run xxx -bench 'GivenPercentileSlice' -benchtime=2000000x` panics at hdr_benchmark_test.go:64 with `index out of range [1000000] with length 1000000`. Note the correctly-written sibling benchmark BenchmarkHistogramValueAtPercentile (line 43) makes quantiles of len totalDatapoints and indexes data[i%totalDatapoints], confirming the intended pattern and the copy-paste defect. Severity medium not high: it corrupts/aborts only the benchmark harness (not library correctness), but default `go test -bench .` with a 1s benchtime can drive b.N past 1e6 for these cheap ops, so a normal benchmark run crashes.

result.plan:
# hdrhistogram-go — Next-Iteration Hardening Plan

Baseline: **85.9% coverage, zero fuzzing.** The C sibling ships ClusterFuzzLite; this port ships nothing. The findings below prove **6 memory-safety panics reachable from the public `Decode`/log-reader APIs on untrusted input**, plus **4 numeric/interop correctness bugs**. This plan closes them, adds native fuzzing, and lifts coverage on the exact lines that mask these defects.

Legend: **[C]** = CONFIRMED (proven by execution), **[P]** = PLAUSIBLE.

---

## 1. Confirmed bugs (deduped) — fix these first

### Group A — `Decode()` panics on untrusted input (DoS). All in `hdr_encoding.go`.
The single most concentrated risk surface: `Decode` is the public deserialization entry point (log files / network), and **three distinct inputs crash the process**. Many findings converge here.

| # | Severity | Site | Failing input | Panic | Fix |
|---|----------|------|---------------|-------|-----|
| A1 | **high** | `Decode:59` `rbuf := bytes.NewBuffer(decoded[0:8])` | `Decode([]byte(""))` or any base64 decoding to <8 bytes (`"QUJD"`→3B) | `slice bounds out of range [:8] with capacity N` | After the base64 err check, before the slice: `if len(decoded) < 8 { err = fmt.Errorf("encoded histogram too short: got %d bytes, need at least 8", len(decoded)); return }` |
| A2 | **high** | `Decode:76` `decoded[8:8+lengthOfCompressedContents]` | valid cookie `0x1c849314` + `int32(-1)` length | `slice bounds out of range [8:7]` | Change guard at :72 to `if lengthOfCompressedContents < 0 || lengthOfCompressedContents > decodeLengthOfCompressedContents {` (matches C ref `compressed_length < 0`) |
| A3 | **high** | `fillCountsArrayFromSourceBuffer:216` → `hdr.go:setCountAtIndex:315` (`h.counts[idx] += n`, no guard) | zlib-valid V2 blob with more positive varints than `countsLen` (100000×`0x02` into `New(1,1000,3)`) | `index out of range [2048] with length 2048` | Bounds-check inside the loop, mirroring C `apply_to_counts_zz`: reject `dstIndex >= len(rh.counts)` on a positive write and `dstIndex+zerosCount > len(rh.counts)` on a zero-run, returning a "corrupt histogram" error |
| A4 | **low** | `decodeCompressedFormat:181` / `decodeDeCompressedHeaderFormat:259` (`decompressedSlice[0:40]`, no len check) | zlib stream decompressing to <40 bytes | No panic today (io.ReadAll cap≥512) but header is parsed from **zeroed capacity** → misleading "encoding not supported got 0" error | After `io.ReadAll`: `if len(decompressedSlice) < headerSize { err = fmt.Errorf("decompressed histogram truncated: got %d, need %d", len(decompressedSlice), headerSize); return }` |

A1's guard also fixes the log-reader panic in Group B (B2) since that path funnels through `Decode`.

### Group B — log reader panics on malformed logs. `log_reader.go`.

| # | Severity | Site | Failing input | Panic | Fix |
|---|----------|------|---------------|-------|-----|
| B1 | **high** | `decodeNextIntervalHistogram:128` `tag = line[4:commaPos]` | `"Tag=abc\n"` (no comma → `commaPos == -1`) | `slice bounds out of range [:-1]` | In the `HasPrefix(line,"Tag=")` block: `if commaPos < 0 { continue }` before slicing |
| B2 | **high** | `decodeNextIntervalHistogram:187` → `Decode:59` | `"1.0,2.0,3.0,\n"` (regex group 4 `(.*)` matches empty payload) | Same as A1 | Fixed by A1's guard |

### Group C — numeric / interop correctness. `hdr.go`, `log_writer.go`.

| # | Severity | Site | Failing input | Wrong result | Fix |
|---|----------|------|---------------|--------------|-----|
| C1 | **high** | `hdr.go:Mean():229` int64 `count*value` product before float cast | `New(1,1e15,3)`, `RecordValues(1e15, 1e5)` | product wraps → `Mean()` ~10× low, silent | `mean += float64(i.countAtIdx) * float64(h.medianEquivalentValue(i.valueFromIdx)) / totalCount` (StdDev already does this) |
| C2 | **high** | `hdr.go:getNormalizingIndexOffset:169` returns constant `1`; serialized at encoding | any encode | wire header carries offset=1 with **unshifted** counts → C/Java readers shift every bucket by one | `return 0` (Go decoder ignores the field; backward-compatible) |
| C3 | **high** | `log_writer.go:123` `"#[Basetime: ...` (lowercase t) vs reader regex `#\[BaseTime:` | `OutputBaseTime` then read back | base time lost, wrong absolute timestamps | `"#[BaseTime: %d (seconds since epoch)]\n"` |
| C4 | **medium** | `hdr.go:ValueAtPercentile:343` etc. `countAtPercentile=0` at p0 | 100×`RecordValue(777)`, `ValueAtPercentile(0)` | returns `0`, not `Min()=777` (contract + Java divergence) | clamp `if countAtPercentile < 1 { countAtPercentile = 1 }` in all three variants (only when totalCount>0) |
| C5 | **medium** | `hdr.go:ValueAtPercentiles:423` clamp mutates loop-local copy | `ValueAtPercentiles([150])` | returns `map[100:0 150:50015]` — phantom key + illegal key | write clamp back: `if percentiles[i] > 100 { percentiles[i] = 100 }`; optionally `< 0 → 0` |
| C6 | **medium** | `ValueAtPercentile`/`ValueAtPercentiles` clamp only high end (`Slice` variant clamps both) | negative percentile | inconsistent siblings (`-5`→511 vs `Slice`→0) | add `else if percentile < 0 { percentile = 0 }` to the two high-only variants |
| C7 | **low** | `hdr.go:RecordValues` no sign check on `n` | `RecordValues(500, -1)` | returns nil, drives `TotalCount` negative, corrupts all stats | after `countsIndexFor`: `if n < 0 { return fmt.Errorf("cannot record negative count %d", n) }` |
| C8 | **low** | `hdr.go:Reset:258` clears counts+totalCount only | `SetTag/SetStartTimeMs` then `Reset` | stale tag/start/end serialized on reuse (doc says "original state") | zero `h.tag/startTimeMs/endTimeMs` in `Reset` |

### Group D — DoS by resource amplification / silent data loss.

| # | Severity | Site | Detail | Fix |
|---|----------|------|--------|-----|
| D1 | **medium** | `decodeCompressedFormat` → `hdr.go:New:138` | attacker header `sig=5, highest=MaxInt64` forces ~47 MB alloc from a 56-byte input (~880,000× amplification); no tie between geometry and payload size | before `New`, probe geometry and reject `countsLen > actualPayloadLen` (each count/zero-run token consumes ≥1 payload byte — sound tight bound) |
| D2 | **medium** | `log_reader.go:96-103` | final interval line **without trailing `\n`** is silently dropped (EOF branch ignores buffered `line`) | on `io.EOF` with non-empty `line`, fall through and process it; empty → break |
| D3 | **medium** | `hdr_benchmark_test.go:61-87` | dead `data[i]` fill loop sized by `b.N` indexes a fixed 1e6 slice → `go test -bench .` panics `index out of range [1000000]` once b.N>1e6 | delete both dead fill loops; change `h, data :=` → `h, _ :=` |

### Group E — thread-safety contract (not a code defect; document + guard).

| # | Severity | Detail | Action |
|---|----------|--------|--------|
| E1 | **medium** | `window.go:Rotate` races `w.Current` pointer (window.go:43) and `counts[]`/`totalCount` (Reset vs setCountAtIndex) — proven under `-race`. Library-wide: even two `RecordValue` goroutines race. No `sync`/`atomic` anywhere. | Document `Histogram`/`WindowedHistogram` are NOT safe for concurrent use (README + godoc). A real fix (atomic `Current` + synchronized counters, or fresh-histogram swap in Rotate) is a design change — park it. |

---

## 2. Fuzzing

**Verdict: add Go 1.18 native fuzz targets AND wire ClusterFuzzLite like the C port.** Every finding in Group A/B/D was found by hand; a fuzzer would have caught all of them in seconds. The port has zero `Fuzz*` functions today.

### Targets to add

| FuzzXxx | Entry point | Property asserted | Priority |
|---------|-------------|-------------------|----------|
| `FuzzDecode` | `Decode([]byte)` | never panics on arbitrary bytes; successful decode → stable re-encode/re-decode (TotalCount preserved) | **P0** — guards A1–A4, D1 |
| `FuzzLogReader` | `NewHistogramLogReader().NextIntervalHistogram` | parsing untrusted text never panics and terminates | **P0** — guards B1, B2, D2 |
| `FuzzRecordEncodeDecode` | `New`+`RecordValue`+`Encode`+`Decode` | generative round-trip preserves `TotalCount` and `ValueAtQuantile(99)` (reaches the decoder hot path that raw bytes rarely reach) | **P1** — semantic guard, exercises C2 |
| `FuzzZigZagRoundTrip` | `zig_zag_encode_i64`/`decode` | `decode(encode(v)) == v`, `n == len(buf)` | P2 — regression net for LEB128 ladder |
| `FuzzZigZagDecodeBytes` | `zig_zag_decode_i64([]byte)` | never panics; `0 ≤ n ≤ min(len,9)`; canonical re-encode ≤ consumed | P2 |
| `FuzzImport` | `Import(*Snapshot)` | no panic on caller geometry; `TotalCount ≥ 0`; `len(counts)==countsLen` | P3 (defense-in-depth) |
| `FuzzLogRoundTrip` | writer→reader | anything the writer emits, the reader parses back | P2 — **already fails today** on tag `","` (writer regex `.[, \r\n].` accepts it, reader chokes); fix writer to `strings.ContainsAny(tag, ", \r\n")` |
| `FuzzDecodeMemoryCeiling` | `Decode` | decode alloc ≤ 8 MB for ≤4 KB input | P3 — guards D1 |

### Ready-to-commit harnesses (top 3)

**`hdr_encoding_fuzz_test.go`**
```go
package hdrhistogram

import "testing"

func FuzzDecode(f *testing.F) {
	f.Add([]byte(""))
	f.Add([]byte("QUJD"))
	if h := New(1, 1000, 3); h != nil {
		h.RecordValue(42)
		if enc, err := h.Encode(V2CompressedEncodingCookieBase); err == nil {
			f.Add(enc)
		}
	}
	f.Fuzz(func(t *testing.T, data []byte) {
		// PROPERTY 1: Decode never panics on arbitrary bytes.
		h, err := Decode(data)
		if err != nil {
			return
		}
		// PROPERTY 2: a successfully decoded histogram re-round-trips stably.
		enc, err := h.Encode(V2CompressedEncodingCookieBase)
		if err != nil {
			t.Fatalf("re-encode failed: %v", err)
		}
		h2, err := Decode(enc)
		if err != nil {
			t.Fatalf("re-decode failed: %v", err)
		}
		if h2.TotalCount() != h.TotalCount() {
			t.Fatalf("totalCount drift %d != %d", h2.TotalCount(), h.TotalCount())
		}
	})
}
```

**`log_reader_fuzz_test.go`**

**`fuzz_roundtrip_test.go`**
```go
package hdrhistogram

import "testing"

func FuzzRecordEncodeDecode(f *testing.F) {
	f.Add(int64(1), int64(1000000), uint8(3), int64(42))
	f.Add(int64(1), int64(3600000000), uint8(3), int64(1))
	f.Add(int64(1000), int64(1000000000), uint8(5), int64(999999))

		}
```

### ClusterFuzzLite

**Yes — mirror the C port.** Native Go fuzz targets are OSS-Fuzz/ClusterFuzzLite-compatible via the Go fuzz build support. Add:

## 5. Safety — panic→error conversions and overflow guards

All are one-liners already specified above; consolidated as the safety checklist:
- **Length guards before slicing** (convert panics to errors): A1 `len(decoded) < 8`; A4 `len(decompressedSlice) < headerSize` (also for the V1 40-byte header at :259); B1 `commaPos < 0 → continue`.
- **Signed-field guards:** A2 reject negative `lengthOfCompressedContents`; C7 reject negative `n` in `RecordValues`.
- **Bounds guard in decode loop:** A3 check `dstIndex`/zero-run against `len(rh.counts)` (mirror C `apply_to_counts_zz`, return `HDR_TRAILING_ZEROS_INVALID`-equivalent error).
- **Resource guard:** D1 reject `countsLen > actualPayloadLen` before allocating.
- **Overflow guard:** C1 do `Mean` multiply in `float64` (the int64 `count*value` product wraps at ~9.2e18).
- All conversions must return a descriptive `error`, never panic, since these are public APIs over untrusted input.

Phase 2 removes silent wrong-number/interop-corruption bugs a fuzzer can't see. Phase 3/4 raise coverage past 92% and institutionalize the gains. This is sized for one focused iteration; Phases 1–2 are the non-negotiable core.
result.critic:
Reviewing the plan for completeness, here are the concrete gaps — ordered by value, with the two proposed-fix flaws first since they matter most:

- **D1's guard is unsound and will reject valid input.** `countsLen > actualPayloadLen` breaks legitimate *sparse* histograms: `New(1, 1e12, 3)` with one recorded value has a huge `countsLen` (geometry-driven) but a ~10-byte payload, so the guard fires on good data — replace with an absolute `countsLen`/highest-value geometry ceiling, not a payload tie.
- **C2 fixes only the encode side; the decode side of `normalizingIndexOffset` is never verified.** A C/Java histogram that was rotated ships offset≠0 with shifted counts; if the Go decoder "ignores the field," it misplaces every bucket on read — add a decode test with a nonzero incoming offset (the exact PR #137 bug class) and fix the read path.
- **The auto-resize / out-of-range record path is entirely absent** — neither fuzzed nor covered: recording a value above `highestTrackableValue`, any reallocation, and decode/merge of a resized histogram.
- **"Golden" vectors are still self-oracle.** The uncompressed hex golden pins *this port against itself*; add at least one real C/Java-emitted encoded histogram (the C sibling can produce it) decoded by Go with pinned stats — that is the only true interop guard for C2/C3.
- **No `-race` CI job despite E1** — the concurrency finding is document-only, leaving the race unguarded; add a `go test -race` job plus a concurrent-`RecordValue` stress test.
- **No CI coverage gate** — the plan targets ≥92% but nothing fails the build when coverage regresses; wire an enforced threshold, and confirm the committed `testdata/fuzz` corpus actually runs as unit tests on every PR (not only during nightly fuzzing).
- **`New()` argument validation is untested** — `New(0, …)`, `lo >= hi`, `sig` outside 1–5, and negative bounds are the first untrusted surface before any Decode.
- **`ValueAtQuantile` float-input edges are uncovered** — NaN/Inf/negative/>100 quantile go unhandled while only the integer percentile variants (C4–C6) are addressed.
- **`totalCount` accumulator overflow is unaddressed** — C1 fixes `Mean`'s product, but `RecordValues(v, n)` with large `n` (or long runs) can still wrap the int64 total silently; guard/saturate it.
- **Log-reader edge cases beyond ParseFloat**: Windows `\r\n` endings, legend/version lines, and non-`StartTime`/`BaseTime` comment lines are not enumerated in the branch tests or `FuzzLogReader` seeds.
- **The `FuzzDecodeMemoryCeiling` "alloc ≤ 8 MB" assertion is flaky** — allocation measurement inside a fuzz target is nondeterministic; assert the bounded `countsLen`/geometry ceiling directly instead.

Highest-value single item to do first: **fix D1's bound before implementing it** (it is specified as a fix that will corrupt decoding of legitimate sparse histograms) — an absolute geometry/`countsLen` ceiling, validated by a fuzz seed that is a valid large-range/few-sample histogram, not the payload-ratio check as written.
